candidates.stmt: I’m Babak (but everyone calls me Bobby). I live in Eccles and work as a policy advisor on renewable energy. I’m passionate about lowering bills for households and businesses through clean electricity and believe there is so much more we could be doing in this space locally. I am also committed to securing more social housing for Salford, and making sure new developments are genuinely shaped by local people for the benefit of current residents as well as future ones.

I'm a proud Salfordian. I was born and raised in Lower Broughton, and still live here today - six decades later. I’m a graduate of Salford University, with academic qualifications in environmental sciences BSc (hons) and sustainable energy technologies MSc. I have worked on environmental and energy projects across Europe. I’ve been a Green Party member for well over a decade and been the Blackfriars & Trinity candidate since 2021, and was most recently our General Election candidate for the Worsley & Eccles constituency and our Salford City Mayoral candidate in 2024.

If elected, I will protect renters’ rights in the ward and improve living conditions. I would do this by bringing all property management companies into a licensing scheme, which would hold them to a clear charter to their tenants, with a condition that those companies who are found to consistently fail to meet these standards being fined and then removed from the Approved Management list, denying them the chance to operate other buildings. I will strengthen residents-only parking permissions, to ensure the ward isn’t turned into a car park for commuters to Manchester City Centre and combine bin emptying and road sweeping for cleaner streets.

I’m a long-term resident of Blackfriars & Trinity (including Adelphi & Chapel Wharf & Greengate) having lived in the area for over 20 years and in the North West for more than three decades. I’m proud to be the only candidate in this election who actually lives in the ward and experiences the same day-to-day issues as local residents.

My campaign is built on action, not promises. I started as a volunteer “litter hero” four years ago, tackling the growing waste problem in Greengate following rapid development. Since then, I’ve worked consistently to improve our area—leading campaigns that secured CCTV in Blackfriars Park, pushing for increased police action on anti-social behaviour, and holding the council to account on road repairs and infrastructure.

I currently serve on the East Salford budget and highways committee, where I’ve successfully pushed for urgent action on potholes and road conditions across the ward.

Like many residents, I’m frustrated that despite significant new council tax revenues from local developments, our area has seen little reinvestment. We still face poor road conditions, litter problems, empty retail units, and a lack of essential services like GP and dental provision.

I’m standing for election to change that.

My priorities are simple: cleaner streets, safer neighbourhoods, and proper investment in Blackfriars & Trinity. I believe our community deserves better—and I have a proven track record of getting results.

I am a firm believer that in order to create change on a wider-scale, we all have to work together within our local communities first. I cannot change the opinions of Presidents, but I can do my part to make Salford a better and kinder place to be. One of the ways I currently do this are volunteering for an LGBTQ+ charity to run free sexual health clinic testing. Another way is running to be a councillor for the Green Party, to support grassroots politics rooted in hope.

I am delighted to have been selected as the candidate for Broughton, having lived in Lower Broughton for the past six years. My job is being a field researcher, with my main work involving interviewing bus passengers about their journeys. I am motivated to find out how to improve bus services all over the North West, especially since I go everywhere on public transport myself. One of the things I will push for if elected is a network of radial bus priority routes to assist in providing more reliable bus journeys across Greater Manchester.

A message from Ceewhy Ochoga

I am privileged to be one of your Labour candidates for Cadishead and Lower Irlam ward and I am ready to hit the ground running to represent and support my local community.

I’m not new to representing people. As President of the University of Salford Students’ Union, I was elected to speak up for thousands of students, listen to their concerns, and make sure their voices were heard where decisions were made. That experience shaped how I see public service – it’s about showing up, being accountable, and following through.

Alongside that, I have been involved in community leadership, voluntary work, and local initiatives for a number of years. I understand what it means to work with people, not just for them.

Over the past few weeks I’ve been knocking on doors, attending meetings, and listening. I have met families who care deeply about their neighbourhood, residents who take pride in their streets, and people who simply want to see their community continue to thrive. You’ve told me about potholes damaging your cars, traffic frustrations on local roads, and most of all the feeling that decisions get made at the council without residents being properly asked first. These are not just complaints, they are real concerns that affect daily life.

If you elect me, you’ll see me here. At your events, at your meetings, and at your door. I’ll bring your concerns directly to the council table, and I’ll work hard to protect the strong community spirit that makes Cadishead and Lower Irlam special.

I want to build on that by being a councillor who is genuinely present in the ward. Someone you can reach easily, who listens, and who makes sure your voice is heard at the council.

I’m looking forward to working alongside you and alongside Bill to make real improvements together.

I spent 18 years of my life living in a run down area in the north east where the council weren’t listening and nothing was improving. It felt like we all just had to accept it. After moving to Salford four years ago, a place I’m proud to now call home, I can still see some of those same issues again. This time, I’m not accepting it. People are feeling let down and things aren’t changing. This is why I’m standing. I’m here to listen, take action and make sure things actually improve.

Claremont is a pretty nice place to live - but there's room for improvement.

After gaining a degree in Manchester I worked at a variety of IT companies before joining a housing developer. I'm pretty much retired now and, if elected, would commit to representing Claremont full-time with a focus on making it an even nicer place to live.

Much of the traffic in the area is generated by commuters, anyone who lives on or near Lancaster Road or Claremont Road will know the misery that brings. Having spent almost 40 years on The Height I'd push for measures to be put in place to dissuade commuters from using our streets as rat-runs. Reduced traffic volume and speed would make our streets a safer and more pleasant environment for walking and cycling - with the added benefit of fewer pot holes.

I'm a regular bus user and whilst I appreciate the recent improvements that have been made to public transport, particularly the return of the Night Bus, as a councillor I'd lobby the Bee Network for more frequent services and an occasional 'express' service via Bolton Road that bypassed the Precinct and reduced the journey time into Manchester.

When the sun is out, there's nothing nicer than sitting outside reading a book or just soaking up the sunlight - until a neighbour decides to jet wash their drive or mow their lawn. I'd advocate for a 'quiet Sunday' once a month where people were strongly discouraged from making noise between 11:00am and 9:00pm. I'd also encourage the council to be more proactive in dealing with noise complaints, whether it be persistent barking dogs or off-road motor bikes.

As a long standing Green Party member I'd abide by the Green Party's commitments to the environment and social justice. I'd encourage the building of council and social housing on brownfield sites and push for rent controls and an end to the conversion of family homes into HMOs.

Representing the people of Eccles as your Labour Councillor over the past five years has been the privilege of my life. I'm proud to once again stand to represent the community I was raised in as your Labour candidate.

During this time, I have worked to deliver major regeneration for Eccles. I will continue to ensure residents' voices shape our plans as we develop a masterplan to transform our town, creating a vibrant centre, improving green spaces, and protecting our historic buildings.

We have already begun, with investment in the Town Hall and restoration of the Carnegie Library at the Gateway. I remain committed to making Eccles a better place for everyone.

I have also supported local community groups, especially those helping older residents, through fundraising and volunteering at events such as Eccles VIP activities and the Ellesmere Park Estate Supper Club.

My experience as an academic, trade union representative with GB and UCU, and trustee for local charities has strengthened my ability to stand up for local people.

Over the next four years, I will focus on a greener Eccles, safer communities, stronger local groups, tackling fly-tipping, and improving our roads.

I would be honoured to continue representing you and hope for your support.

Sara grew up in Eccles and still lives locally with her family. She is a mum and has worked in social housing for over 20 years, supporting people with housing, health and financial challenges.

She has always been actively involved in the community — as a Brownie Leader (Little Owl), a member of the Monton Green Primary School PTA, and as a School Governor for four years. These experiences have shaped her strong commitment to supporting children, families and local communities.

Sara is standing as a Green Party candidate because she cares about the area and wants to see Eccles thrive. With regeneration underway, she believes it is important that it benefits the people who already live here — with genuinely affordable homes, support for local businesses, and real opportunities for young people.

Through her work and her own experience, Sara understands how rising costs and financial pressures are affecting households, and how important it is that people have access to affordable housing and the support they need.

She also believes in creating healthier, greener neighbourhoods, with well-maintained, safe and welcoming parks and public spaces — including the Greenway and new green spaces created through regeneration — so that everyone can enjoy where they live. This includes taking concerns about anti-social behaviour seriously and working with local partners to help keep communities safe.

The Green Party focuses its limited resources on a small number of areas, so residents may not see Sara knocking on every door in Eccles this time. However, she is always happy to hear from local people and welcomes anyone getting in touch to share their views or concerns.

Sara believes local politics should focus on listening to residents, thinking long-term, and working together to build a fairer, healthier and more sustainable community.

DELIVERING REAL RESULTS FOR OUR COMMUNITY

I’m Mishal Saeed, your local Councillor for Higher Irlam and Peel Green—and I’m standing for re-election because I believe local politics should be about one thing: getting things done for our community.

I’m proud to call Salford home. It’s where I’ve built my life, started my family, and committed over 15 years to public service—from grassroots volunteering to standing up for workers, families and local communities.

Over the past four years, I’ve focused on delivering real, visible results:

* A new children’s play area at Brookhouse
* Investment in community spaces and local groups
* Funding for schools, grassroots initiatives and local charities
* Action on flooding, antisocial behaviour and road safety
* Improving the quality of roads by resurfacing Brookhouse Avenue, Northfleet Road, Broadway and part of Liverpool Road
* Protecting our area from the A57 lane reduction scheme
* Supporting our allotments and voluntary groups
* Protecting and enhancing our green spaces

I don’t just talk about change—I work with residents to make it happen.

⸻

A LOCAL VOICE THAT LISTENS — AND ACTS

Politics can feel disconnected from everyday life. I do things differently.

Whether it’s holding organisations like United Utilities to account, working with GMP to tackle antisocial behaviour, or standing alongside care workers fighting unfair conditions—I make sure your voice is heard where it matters.

When residents raised concerns about the potential closure of Astley and Monica Court and about the impact of A57 lane reduction scheme, I listened and campaigned—and we won those campaigns together.

That’s what local representation should look like.

⸻

FOCUSED ON WHAT REALLY MATTERS

This election isn’t about national political noise or divisive headlines.

It’s about:

* The condition of your roads
* The safety of your streets
* Investment in our community
* Enhancing our green spaces, play areas and allotments
* Opportunities for our

“A vote for the Greens is a vote for hope”.

You might have heard this line before, you might not, but it is something I firmly believe in. The tide is changing as more people across the country become aware of the Greens’ simple and honest promise to put our communities first. I am standing on that promise.

I work to promote Greater Manchester as a great place to live, work, visit, and invest in. I see opportunity right across this borough, and especially in the pockets that have previously been neglected or forgotten. And I’ve seen places change for the better when people are encouraged to come together and unite.

I know the Greens might not be familiar to some residents of Higher Irlam and Peel Green, but we are here to listen and do the work. With Green Councillors on Salford City Council, we will champion our communities, be proactive, make climate-positive decisions, and push for greater opportunities. Higher Irlam and Peel Green deserves that.

I'm standing as someone outside the political bubble - a working dad of two young children who cares about the kind of place families actually have to live in day to day.

I’m not here to make big promises I can’t keep, and I’m not pretending this is a safe Conservative seat. But I do think residents deserve someone willing to listen properly and speak honestly about what’s going wrong; whether that’s anti-social behaviour, poor planning decisions, or feeling like local voices are ignored.
Labour has held this area for years, and Reform will offer protest. I’m offering something simpler: a fresh set of eyes, a willingness to challenge decisions that don’t make sense, and a focus on the everyday issues people deal with on their own streets.

Even where I can’t win, I can make sure your concerns are heard - and that’s worth doing properly.

Statement of Simon FAN

I'm from Hong Kong, a Christian, a member of the Chartered Institute of Housing and the Chartered Governance Institute.
I believe in practical actions, not ideology.  Local council should focus on services that work and keeping our area clean and safe.
Public service has always been important to me.  In early years, I served in voluntary organisations, e.g. I had joined the Royal Hong Kong Regiment (the volunters) from 1979 to 1986.  Now retired, I can give my full time to serving the community and supporting residents.
Please vote for me on May 7th
.

I am a co-founder and former co-director of a Salford based not for profit organisation Result CIC, having retired in October 2025.

As a personal development coach and training facilitator for over 13 years, I have helped people grow and gain much more confidence in themselves at work and in life in general.

As a former refugee, I have used my own experiences of dealing with racism, as well as other challenges that I have encountered to help others. All these experiences, as well as my varied careers, have given me empathy for the lives of others and the challenges they are faced with.

I believe that the Green Party is creating a wave of much needed focus on what needs to be achieved in this country both locally and nationally. To start personally to help with local issues would be a privilege.

I have been proud to serve as your Councillor for the past four years.

Working with residents and local groups has shown me the true spirit of Little Hulton - a caring community full of talent, resilience and pride. Every day I see people supporting each other and creating new opportunities. That’s the Little Hulton I know and love.

Alongside my role as Councillor, I am a trustee of local charities and Chair of Governors at Star Academy. Over the last four years, I have fought hard to secure more than £2.5 million of investment in our area, including the improvements to Peel Park, Little Hulton District Centre, Warton Playing Fields and Smithfold Field.

I have supported more than 900 residents with issues such as housing, schools, fly-tipping and access to services. Listening to you and speaking up for Little Hulton is one of the most important parts of my job as a Councillor.

If re-elected, I will continue working side by side with residents to make Little Hulton an even better place to live.

I’ve taken the community of Little Hulton to my heart since moving here seven years ago. With our precinct, local shops, small businesses, and services like the community hubs, health centre and library, Little Hulton has real potential, but is too often overlooked.

Through my work as a driving instructor in the area, I’ve spoken to many residents. People are proud of where they’re from, but there’s also a strong feeling that our corner of Salford is ignored and lacks opportunities.

With more Green councillors on Salford City Council, we can change that. We can focus on what Little Hulton needs. That means creating more opportunities for residents, including improving connections to Manchester, Bolton and across Salford, and keeping our streets clean, safe and welcoming.

Little Hulton deserves attention, investment, and pride. Together, we can build a community we’re all proud to call home.

I am Anne Fasan,  representing my ward in little Hulton demanding for a better life for local residents. I spent years representing, working and fighting for the less privilege, advocating for a safer community and with less crime and antisocial behaviour. 
I am an entrepreneur and keen about growing the local community business and tackling poverty and crime at all levels.  Create space for our young people and children. Improving transport and local infrastructure. I am a local resident of little Hulton and 20 years living here, demanding a change from current government, holding them accountable for ignoring little Hulton for a long time.

Having been a student, retail worker, and small business owner, I have first-hand experience of the pressures ordinary people face. Politics has become distant and unaccountable, allowing damaging decisions to go unchecked. Both local and national government must serve the people, not powerful interests. If elected, I will be transparent, accountable and take action on local issues such as antisocial behaviour, littering and access to essential services, standing firmly with the community.

I will:

- Oppose austerity and invest in our public services
- Protect and expand urban green spaces
- Improve parking and transport links for residents

I've lived in Salford all my life.  I grew up in Weaste until I was 11 years old and my mum and dad's house was demolished as part of a compulsory purchase scheme.  At which point we moved into Swinton.

I started my working career as a papergirl and went onto a Saturday and after-school job in retail on the once vibrant Swinton Shopping centre  and commenced on YTS (Youth Training Scheme - £27.50 a week in 1985 equivalent of an apprenticeship in today's world) when I left High School.

Throughout my career I  progressed into management positions prior to having my children.

I am a mother of 2 boys, one aged 19 and the other is 16 years old.

After the birth of my youngest child,  I undertook a law degree as a mature student,  whilst cleaning for a living, often studying well into the early hours of the morning.

I co-own a small craft ale bar in Swinton which is very much community focussed.  We have a walking club, book club and football club and raise much needed funds for various charities as a team.

I believe that the labour party have become complacent and consider Salford a safe place to hide.

The council have wasted far too much of our council tax money on the  Salford sports stadium, whilst ignoring the real issues faced by local residents.

Fly tipping,  roads are riddled with pot holes which often results in costly repairs for motorists whilst the council  insists it is resurfacing roads and highways.  Unacceptable waiting lists for social housings, HMOs in abundance  and general apathy.

I am standing to support change in our local community.  A change that makes your voice heard, that makes your opinions matter and more importantly makes your lives better.

The communities of Pendleton and Charlestown has so many amazing features from our local shops, small businesses, community hubs, schools, and vital services like health centres and libraries, Pendleton and Charlestown have real potential — but too often feel overlooked.

I've lived in the nearby area for a few years and have had the chance to speak with many local residents across the area. There’s a strong sense of pride in where people live, but also a shared feeling that these communities don’t always get the attention, investment, or opportunities they deserve.

With more Green councillors on Salford City Council, we can change that. We can focus on what Pendleton and Charlestown need — from improving connections across Salford and into Manchester, to supporting local businesses and ensuring our streets are clean, safe, and welcoming for everyone.

Pendleton and Charlestown deserves to be heard, valued, and invested in. Together, we can build stronger, fairer communities that we’re all proud to call home.

I work for a local charity that brings people together through community meals made out of food that would otherwise go to waste.

I am standing as your Green Party candidate because I believe our community deserves a fairer and greener future. Through my work, I see first-hand both the amount of good food that goes to waste and the growing number of people affected by rising living costs and social isolation.

Helping create welcoming spaces where people can share meals has shown me the real difference community-led action can make. This experience has given me a strong understanding of the challenges facing many residents, as well as the importance of practical, local solutions. I believe in listening to people, working collaboratively, and focusing on what will genuinely improve everyday life.

I am standing with the Green Party because I believe there needs to be a stronger focus on fairness, sustainability, and community wellbeing. Being able to contribute to improving local issues in Salford Quays would be a privilege.

I’ve been a **proud resident of the Quays for over
three years now**, and have decided to stand as
your Labour candidate in the local elections. I
want the Quays to have a Councillor who is
visible, accountable, and shares the values and
aims of our growing community.

Speaking to fellow residents around the Quays I
hear the same concerns and priorities that I have
myself: clean streets and waterways; growth and
investment that puts residents first; decent and
affordable housing; public services fit to meet an
increasing demand and more action to bring
down anti-social behaviour.

Specifically, I'm asking for you to vote for me so I can deliver on 4 main issues.

1. A cleaner and safer
 Quays
2. More investment in public services
3. Development that puts residents first
4. A Quays Councillor for Quays residents

Residents across the Quays want action, and I will
be a voice on the council that delivers that action
and puts the Quays first in Salford.

Specifically

I am a local NHS community nurse and work within varied roles within the NHS, serving the whole of Salford and surrounding areas at home and in the hospital. I want to see the community that I work in thriving and making real change that is right for the people of Swinton and Wardley.

I volunteer for the charity Two Brews when I am able to fit some time in and work with them on outreach in the city centre which is something I am very passionate about.

My recent pivot in to standing as a council candidate was looking at the world, then our country and then Salford and asking how can I help make a difference? If not now, when? And if not me, then who?

As a nurse I am very adept at talking to people and explaining things with a kind and caring manner. I am confident talking to anyone, personable and relatable and know most of the families in my area either from passing them in the street or caring for them or family members.

I will work hard on local issues such as the state of our roads, crime reduction and supporting our young people. By working together to create a safer place to live, we will ensure that as a community we can all thrive.

I believe in honesty and transparency, and my life and background have given me real, practical experience of listening to people, solving problems and making the most responsible decisions in the best interests of people. The residents of Swinton and Wardley deserve a councillor who is visible, approachable and someone who is willing to stand up for their needs.

I would like the chance to be your local councillor and represent Swinton and Wardley to the best of my abilities to ensure that the Salford that we know and love is not lost. I care deeply about the people of Salford and would love the opportunity to represent your interests and needs.

Hi folks!

You can see my leaflet attached but here are my four main points.

1. Cleaner streets, led by the community - I will tackle litter and dog fouling through community action - including free dog poo bags at problem spots and local clean-ups. Working with residents we can keep Swinton Park clean, safe and welcoming.

2. Back Swinton Precinct and local shops - I will champion our local shops by pushing for better investments, footfall, and support for small businesses. A thriving precinct means jobs, services, and a stronger local economy that works for residents, not absentee interests.

3. Residents first on Swinton Park Golf Course - The future of the golf course must be decided by the local people. I will fight for genuine community consultation, transparency, and a residents-led outcome, because the future of this land belongs to the community and their voices must come first.

4. Fixing potholes and improving our roads - I will push for faster pothole repairs and lasting fixes. With 5,480 repairs last year and £855,000 committed, Swinton Park must receive its fair share for long-term improvements.

Message me at karlrobson4swintonpark@gmail.com if you want more information.

Thank you

We all deserve better. I've always been passionate about helping people around me, with my work requiring me to have strategic and economic skills. I want to share these skills with the local community. If elected, I will improve communication between the community and the council to fix issues, such as potholes, lighting, and cleanliness and be readily available to speak to residents in monthly surgeries.
The Greens are offering two things that have been absent from our institutions for a very, very long time. Hope and togetherness. Vote Green to make hope normal again in Walkden North!

I have lived in Salford for 30 years since leaving university and moving here for work. I spent 15 years supporting people with mental health conditions and saw first-hand how factors such as poverty and poor housing contributed to mental ill health.

I have seen food banks become a part of everyday life for many people in Salford and the number of people including families living in temporary or unsuitable housing has only increased.

I joined the Green Party last year because I wanted to try and do something about the continuing inequality I see around me every day. With the Green Party's help we can make Salford a fairer and more equal place for everyone.

Hello everyone I'm Craig 38 years old from Walkden. I have lived in Walkden since February 2020 and have worked at Tesco Walkden since March 2024. Before moving to Walkden, I lived on Broomhall Road in Swinton with my wife Hannah.

I have been married for 12 years this August. 
I am a people's person, always putting others before myself. I know alot say that, but it is true.

In January 2024 I suffered a blood clot at the back of the brain, which caused me to have a mini stroke. Thankfully my former colleagues at G4S saved my life as too Salford Royal. Sadly i had to leave G4S due to my health. I was later diagnosed with a hole in the heart, which needed to be closed. Before that procedure, I ran as parliamentary candidate on July 4th 2024 putting the people first before my health. Yes it probably wasn't wise, but I wanted to put the people first.

In February 2025 I had the surgery and it was a success. I would personally like to thank Dr.Chaudry, Dr.contractor and the stroke team for me being here today, to carry on helping you, the people.

There are people out there far worse than myself and I will continue helping others for as long as I can.

God bless to you all and thank you

I’m standing for the Green Party because I want to help build a council that works for you. I believe local politics should be practical, compassionate and focused on improving everyday life for the people who live here. That means listening properly to residents, supporting those in need and putting forward-thinking policies in place that help our whole community thrive.

Our area should be somewhere everyone has the chance to do well, where local services are valued, neighbourhoods are supported and people feel heard. I want to see a fairer, greener and more caring community, with honest representation and a real commitment to building the best possible future for all. It would be a privilege to serve local residents and help make our area an even better place to live.

I am Salford born and bred and proud of it.
I have been a councillor for 35 years working on behalf of people like yourselves. A strong trade unionist. A member of the GMB.
As a councillor I have fought hard for the communities of the wards I have represented and would do the same in Weaste and Seedley Ward, working closely with all local residents.
MY PLEDGES TO YOU ARE:
..to work for a cleaner and safer environment
..to work with police and yourselves to lower crime in the area
..to help children and young people in Weaste and Seedley Ward achieve their full potential
..to help improve the health and well-being and social care for the people of Weaste and Seedley Ward.
..to continue to support all communities in Weaste and Seedley Ward in line with Salford’s City of Sanctuary status
..and most importantly to listen to your own views and aspirations for the ward.
I am asking that you use your vote for me on election day Thursday May 7th.

I am standing as a candidate to represent Worsley & Westwood Park because I believe local voices should be heard and acted upon.

I have lived in Worsley for 20 years and have seen significant changes—from rising house prices and increasing local taxes to rapid population growth without the matching expansion in services and infrastructure.

My whole working life has been the privilege of serving the community within the NHS. As a GP in a deprived area of Bolton, I see first hand the struggles ordinary people face every day. This includes delays in GP appointments due to lack of capacity, and the challenges caused by poor coordination between GP and hospital services, which can make it harder for patients to receive timely and joined-up care.

These experiences have shaped my commitment to strengthening healthcare access and ensuring services keep pace with growing communities. I want to continue serving by helping improve the lives of local people in any way possible, working with integrity and dedication for the Worsley community.
